paper: MemSlides: A Hierarchical Memory Driven Agent Framework for Personalized Slide Generation with Multi-turn Local Revision

metadata.description: Ranked research seeds derived from MemSlides' limitations, unexplored edges, and adjacent open problems in personalized presentation generation.
ranking_criteria: Practical impact — How many users/systems would benefit?
Novelty gap — How underexplored is this direction?
Feasibility — Can a meaningful minimal experiment be designed?
Measurability — Can results be clearly evaluated?
Synergy with MemSlides — Builds on or extends the framework

ranked_seeds.seed_name: Cold-Start Profile Induction from Minimal Feedback
ranked_seeds.rationale: MemSlides assumes pre-existing profile memory. In practice, new users have no history. Learning accurate profiles from zero prior knowledge (or very few interactions) is critical for real-world deployment.
ranked_seeds.gap: Profile cold start is explicitly called out as a limitation (§9). No evaluation of few-shot or zero-shot profile initialization exists.

suggested_validation_experiment.design: A/B comparison: (A) MemSlides with empty profile vs. (B) MemSlides with profiles induced from N interactions (N = 1, 3, 5). Use the multi-persona profile bank for ground truth. Measure coverage of ground-truth preferences and persona-alignment scores.
metrics: Number of turns to converge to baseline profile performance
suggested_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: Convergence within 3-5 interactions for common preference dimensions (theme, layout), slower for nuanced dimensions (content specificity, visual style gradations).
suggested_validation_experiment.estimated_effort: 2-4 weeks (build profile induction prompts + evaluation pipeline)

ranked_seeds.seed_name: Implicit Preference Conflict Detection from Revision Behavior
ranked_seeds.rationale: The current reconciliation function ℛ only handles explicit conflicts (request contradicted by profile). Users may subtly contradict profile items through indirect revision behavior, leading to gradual profile drift or user frustration.
ranked_seeds.gap: Implicit conflict detection is not addressed. When a user repeatedly edits generated slides in ways that silently contradict stored preferences, the system has no mechanism to detect or adapt.

suggested_validation_experiment.design: Construct a test suite where user behavior systematically deviates from profile (e.g., profile says 'formal academic template', user repeatedly switches to 'modern minimalist'). Measure: (a) detection rate of silent conflicts by an explicit conflict monitor vs. baseline monitoring; (b) time-to-detection.
metrics: User satisfaction delta after detection + adaptation
suggested_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: Explicit monitors can detect ~60-70% of implicit conflicts within 2 turns; full detection requires behavioral pattern analysis across 3+ turns.
suggested_validation_experiment.estimated_effort: 4-8 weeks (behavior tracking + conflict classifier + controlled user study)

ranked_seeds.seed_name: Strict Verification Improvement via Multi-Agent Consensus
ranked_seeds.rationale: The strict verification rate of 0.534 (with tool memory) is a clear weak point. Automated verification of slide edits is hard because correctness is subjective and context-dependent. Multi-agent consensus (multiple verifier agents debating or voting) may improve reliability.
ranked_seeds.gap: Single-verifier ceiling at ~53% strict verification. Prior work in LLM self-verification shows multi-agent approaches can improve accuracy (e.g., Du et al. 2023, collaborative debate).

suggested_validation_experiment.design: Compare variants: (A) single verifier LLM, (B) verifier with 3-agent consensus voting, (C) verifier with adversarial debate (proposer vs. critic vs. arbiter). Test on the diagnostic matched-pair modify task from the paper.
metrics: Verification when ground truth is known
suggested_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: 3-agent consensus would improve strict verify to ~0.65-0.70; adversarial debate may reach ~0.75-0.80 but with 3× time cost.
suggested_validation_experiment.estimated_effort: 3-6 weeks (build multi-agent verifier + test suite from paper's modify pairs)

ranked_seeds.seed_name: Tool Memory Transfer Across Presentation Platforms
ranked_seeds.rationale: MemSlides' tool memory captures execution experience for its own slide engine. Real-world users work across PowerPoint, Google Slides, Keynote, and web-based tools. Cross-platform transfer of tool memory would dramatically increase practical value.
ranked_seeds.gap: Tool memory is platform-specific in the current implementation. No investigation of transfer learning across presentation tool interfaces.

suggested_validation_experiment.design: Build a platform abstraction layer mapping tool operations (write, inspect, patch, style) to 2-3 target platforms (e.g., python-pptx for PowerPoint, Google Slides API, Reveal.js for web). Measure: (a) tool memory reuse rate across platforms, (b) adaptation cost per new platform, (c) edit completion accuracy on each platform.
metrics: Time to first successful edit on new platform
suggested_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: Operation-scope tool chains (abstract reasoning patterns) transfer well (~70% reuse); round-scope task experience (platform-specific commands) transfers poorly (~20% reuse).

ranked_seeds.seed_name: Personalized Slide Generation with Multi-Modal Memory
ranked_seeds.rationale: MemSlides currently handles text and structural slide content. Many slide decks rely on charts, diagrams, embedded media, and data visualizations. Extending memory to cover multi-modal elements would expand personalization to richer presentation types.
ranked_seeds.gap: No multi-modal personalization memory in current framework. Users cannot store preferences for chart types, color palettes, data visualization styles, or media embeddings.

suggested_validation_experiment.design: Extend user profile memory with multi-modal dimensions (chart type preference, data visualization style, image selection criteria). Create a multi-modal profile bank (3 personas, 3 intents). Measure persona alignment on multi-modal slide generation.
suggested_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: Multi-modal profiles would improve chart-type alignment by ~1.5-2.0 points on a 10-point scale compared to text-only profile baselines.
suggested_validation_experiment.estimated_effort: 8-12 weeks (multi-modal profile schema + generation support + evaluation bank)

ranked_seeds.seed_name: Privacy-Preserving Profile Sharing Across User Cohorts
ranked_seeds.rationale: If MemSlides is deployed at scale, the system learns profiles for many users. Privacy-preserving profile sharing (e.g., differential privacy, federated profile consolidation) could enable cold-start personalization for new users by leveraging cohort patterns.
ranked_seeds.gap: No exploration of multi-user profile sharing or privacy-preserving personalization. The current framework treats each user's profile in isolation.

suggested_validation_experiment.design: Simulate a user cohort with N personas, each with M users. Compare: (A) isolated profiles, (B) k-anonymized cohort profile templates, (C) differentially-private cohort template (ε = 1, 5, 10). Measure template utility for cold-start users.
metrics: Cold-start persona alignment (cohort template vs. isolated)
suggested_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: Cohort templates provide ~50-60% of isolated profile benefit during cold start with ε ≥ 5; tradeoff sharpens below ε = 1.

ranked_seeds.seed_name: Intent Drift Detection in Long-Term Personalization
ranked_seeds.rationale: User preferences evolve. A graduate student who becomes a professor will have different presentation needs. Current MemSlides has no mechanism to detect when a user's intent has fundamentally shifted or when multiple intents have merged.
ranked_seeds.gap: Static profile buckets by intent. No detection of intent drift or evolution over time.

suggested_validation_experiment.design: Simulate user evolution scenarios over 20+ sessions: (A) gradual intent drift (academic → industry talk), (B) abrupt intent change (new project), (C) intent merge (teaching + research). Measure detection accuracy and profile adaptation quality.
metrics: User satisfaction after vs. before adaptation
suggested_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: Gradual drift detection is harder (requires 5+ sessions); abrupt changes are detectable within 1-2 sessions via revision behavior divergence.
suggested_validation_experiment.estimated_effort: 6-10 weeks (intent drift simulation + detection module + longitudinal evaluation)

ranked_seeds.seed_name: Optimal Planning for Non-Linear Revision Sequences
ranked_seeds.rationale: MemSlides handles discrete feedback → edit turns. But real slide authoring involves non-linear revision: users may want to undo an earlier change, branch from a previous version, or batch multiple pending changes into one coherent edit. The Plan phase could support trip planning over edit dependencies.
ranked_seeds.gap: The current framework assumes a linear sequence of independent revision turns. No investigation of edit dependency graphs or transactional batch operations.

suggested_validation_experiment.design: Extend Plan to accept a dependency graph of pending changes. Compare: (A) sequential per-turn edits (current), (B) dependency-aware batched edits. Build a test suite of 10 multi-turn revision scenarios with known dependency structures.
metrics: Number of edit turns to convergence
suggested_validation_experiment.expected_outcome: Dependency-aware batching reduces turn count by 30-50% on scenarios with 3+ pending interdependent changes; no significant difference on single-turn edits.
suggested_validation_experiment.estimated_effort: 4-8 weeks (dependency graph planner + batch execution + test suite)

recommended_first_steps: Rank 1 (Cold-Start Profile Induction) is the most impactful near-term extension — directly addresses a recognized limitation, requires modest effort, and has clear evaluation methodology using the existing profile bank.
Rank 3 (Strict Verification via Multi-Agent Consensus) addresses the clearest numerical weakness (0.534 strict verify) and could produce immediate quality improvements.
Rank 4 (Cross-Platform Tool Memory Transfer) has the highest practical impact potential but requires significant infrastructure investment.
